jokes.text: Mnh. God, I'm gonna go down a full shoe size after this.
jokes.setup: We open on Ilana apparently receiving some kind of foot treatment, context unclear at first.
jokes.punchline: The claim that a foot treatment will reduce her shoe size by a full size — grotesquely exaggerated self-satisfaction about foot care.
jokes.explanation: A solid cold-open joke that drops the audience into an absurd situation with no preamble. The exaggeration (a FULL shoe size) is funny and very Ilana — she's treating mundane foot maintenance as a major body transformation. Economy is its strength. Doesn't hit hard but effectively sets the episode's tone.

jokes.text: I was just cleaning up my bunions. / I think that's actually a callus. Right, girl?
jokes.setup: Ilana greets her friend enthusiastically while simultaneously doing foot maintenance.
jokes.punchline: Ilana casually misidentifies her own foot condition; Abbi corrects her with breezy nonchalance as if this is a totally normal conversational opener.
jokes.explanation: The joke is the casualness of both participants — Ilana's wrong medical vocabulary and Abbi's bored correction of it. It's funny because no normal person would open a social visit this way, but for these two it's completely ordinary. Character-specific and efficient.

jokes.text: It's staring at me.
jokes.setup: Ilana has been discussing her bunion/callus, and the camera (or Abbi's eyeline) lands on the grotesque foot.
jokes.punchline: Abbi's deadpan horror — she anthropomorphizes the foot condition, implying it's so large and protuberant it seems sentient.
jokes.explanation: Three words, maximum effect. The anthropomorphization of a callus is genuinely weird and funny. Its economy is its best quality — it doesn't explain itself, just drops the image. Abbi's dry delivery of disgust is a good straight-man moment.

jokes.text: Flowers?! In a vase?!
jokes.setup: A friend brings Ilana hydrangeas from their garden, presented in a vase — a perfectly normal, thoughtful gift.
jokes.punchline: Ilana reacts as if this is an almost incomprehensibly luxurious and sophisticated gesture — flowers in a vase being treated like a Fabergé egg.
jokes.explanation: This is a tight, well-observed character joke. The two exclamations — 'Flowers?!' and 'In a vase?!' — are perfectly structured; the second escalates the absurdity. It works because it's entirely character-consistent: Ilana lives such a chaotic, broke, urban life that someone putting flowers in a vase feels like an aristocratic act. The economy is exceptional. Close to an 8 — it's highly quotable and lands on Ilana's specific worldview.

jokes.text: 'Cause I could really use some help tackling my ingrowns. It's honestly a three-person job.
jokes.setup: Ilana asks what her guests are up to today, inviting them to hang out.
jokes.punchline: She immediately pivots to requesting help with her ingrown toenails, and the escalation is that she's already assessed this as requiring a full team of three.
jokes.explanation: 'It's honestly a three-person job' is the key line — the confidence and practicality with which Ilana treats her ingrown toenails as a logistical challenge requiring workforce planning is a perfect character beat. Very Ilana to have done the triage already. Good structure: the setup is casual sociality, the punchline is deeply unglamorous specificity.

jokes.text: Johnny, would you be a pal and towel me up? I'm a little raw!
jokes.setup: Ilana casually asks her friend Johnny to help dry her post-foot-treatment feet.
jokes.punchline: The TMI qualifier 'I'm a little raw!' — the cheerful unselfconsciousness of asking someone to towel her raw feet is delightfully shameless.
jokes.explanation: The joke is Ilana's total lack of embarrassment — she's demanding intimate foot-care assistance with the casual tone of asking someone to pass the salt. 'A little raw' is a good detail that makes it more viscerally uncomfortable. Character-specific but not especially novel at this point in the cold open.

jokes.text: No, it's just an old handkerchief I found lying around. That old thing?
jokes.setup: Someone compliments Ilana's outfit.
jokes.punchline: She dismisses it with mock humility as 'just an old handkerchief,' and Abbi's 'That old thing?' punctuates with the classic feigned-nonchalance trope — but the actual garment being a handkerchief means the dismissal is literally true, making it simultaneously humble-brag and genuinely low-rent.
jokes.explanation: The joke plays on the familiar 'oh this old thing?' false-modesty trope, but the twist is that it actually IS a found handkerchief worn as clothing, so the humility is sincere poverty rather than affectation. Good double layer. Abbi's straight-man 'That old thing?' sells it.

jokes.text: Jaimé, I'm, like, smelling something funky. / Mi amora, I don't mean to be mean, but I think it's your disgusting feet. Like, I can taste it.
jokes.setup: Ilana observes a funky smell in the apartment.
jokes.punchline: Jaimé immediately and confidently identifies Ilana's own freshly-treated feet as the source — and escalates to 'I can taste it,' which is viscerally disgusting and very funny.
jokes.explanation: 'I can taste it' is the escalation that makes this land. The structure is clean: Ilana complains about a smell → is immediately roasted by her own roommate as the source → escalation to synesthetic disgust (tasting a smell). Jaimé's 'I don't mean to be mean, but...' faux-diplomatic framing before the brutal honesty is also good comic structure.

jokes.text: My tootsies are gonna be fresh to death by the time you get back, if you choose to come back.
jokes.setup: Ilana has been told her feet smell so bad the guests might not want to return.
jokes.punchline: She promises fresh feet but hedges with 'if you choose to come back' — an almost sincere acknowledgment that the foot smell might reasonably drive people away permanently.
jokes.explanation: 'Fresh to death' is a dated slang phrase that Ilana uses earnestly; 'if you choose to come back' is the honest concession that lands. Functionally funny but not exceptional.

jokes.text: Okay, folks, let's talk about this month's aesthetic. As you know, the theme is... birds... again.
jokes.setup: A workplace meeting at Anthropologie where the manager announces the monthly theme.
jokes.punchline: The defeated pause before 'again' — the store has apparently done birds so many times that 'birds... again' is a punchline in itself, delivered with weary resignation.
jokes.explanation: A perfectly observed satire of Anthropologie's boho-aesthetic repetitiveness. The ellipsis before 'again' does a lot of work — it implies the manager herself is tired of this. Economy is excellent; three words ('birds... again') skewer an entire corporate-creativity culture. The specificity of birds as the repeated motif is smart: Anthropologie genuinely leans into that aesthetic.

jokes.text: It's timeless. And... and timely. Time's Up, right?
jokes.setup: Abbi enthusiastically agrees with the bird theme idea, searching for justifications.
jokes.punchline: She awkwardly pivots from 'timeless/timely' wordplay to trying to connect birds to the #MeToo Time's Up movement — a desperate, confused attempt to seem culturally aware that makes no logical sense.
jokes.explanation: This is sharp workplace comedy. Abbi is visibly overcompensating in a meeting, and the non-sequitur leap from 'timeless/timely' to 'Time's Up, right?' is a pitch-perfect depiction of someone awkwardly trying to seem woke and with-it to their boss while making absolutely no sense. Character-specific: Abbi's earnest people-pleasing desperation is perfectly captured.

jokes.text: Ma'am, where can I find that shirt? / Okay, you can call me 'miss,' and this guy right here is gonna be around the corner at the bottom of the stairs. But if you hit the vanity made of vintage teacups, you've gone too far.
jokes.setup: Abbi is mid-pitch for her idea when a customer interrupts to ask a basic store question.
jokes.punchline: Abbi snaps into associate mode, corrects 'ma'am' to 'miss,' gives detailed directions, and the store's layout detail — 'a vanity made of vintage teacups' — is a perfect satirical jab at Anthropologie's famously bizarre and overwrought interior design.
jokes.explanation: Two jokes in one: (1) the 'ma'am/miss' correction showing Abbi's vanity about her age mid-task-switch, and (2) 'a vanity made of vintage teacups' as the actual landmark in the store — a beautiful specific satire of Anthropologie's aesthetic. The interruption structure (mid-pitch, customer appears) is also well-timed cringe comedy. 'You've gone too far' as wayfinding is inherently funny.

jokes.text: Ma'am, where can I find the, um... / ...the smelly sticks? / Mm. You can call me 'miss.' And you call the smelly sticks 'sage smudging wands,' and they're gonna be over there in the corner, right next to the robin's nest Caucasian headdresses.
jokes.setup: Another customer interruption; this time asking about smelly sticks.
jokes.punchline: Three layers: (1) Abbi again corrects 'ma'am' with robotic consistency, (2) she rebrands 'smelly sticks' with the pretentious retail name 'sage smudging wands,' and most importantly (3) the directional landmark is 'robin's nest Caucasian headdresses' — a sudden, sharp critique of cultural appropriation in bohemian retail, dropped completely deadpan.
jokes.explanation: This is the best of the Anthropologie sequence and one of the episode's stronger jokes. It operates as a callback to joke 15, but the punchline here is significantly sharper: 'robin's nest Caucasian headdresses' is a three-word indictment of cultural appropriation in boho retail, delivered as an utterly mundane directional. The specificity ('Caucasian headdresses' — i.e., headdresses made for white people to buy) is incisive cultural criticism disguised as a wayfinding instruction. The callback structure (ma'am/miss again) earns extra comedy through repetition. Genuinely clever writing.

jokes.text: I would be honored to lead this project.
jokes.setup: Abbi has just pitched her Gershwin idea and is still mid-sales-associate-interruption cycle.
jokes.punchline: The formal dignity of 'I would be honored' is tonally absurd for pitching a retail window display at Anthropologie — she speaks as if accepting a diplomatic appointment.
jokes.explanation: The humor is in the tonal mismatch — Abbi's formal gravitas vs. the absurdly low stakes of the situation. Good character work: her desperation to be taken seriously inflates the register. Brief and efficient.

jokes.text: You're not ready yet. Just doesn't happen overnight.
jokes.setup: Abbi has just volunteered with formal dignity to lead the window display project.
jokes.punchline: Lisa's dismissal is almost comically brief — 'You're not ready yet' treating Abbi like a Jedi padawan, not an adult who wants to dress a shop window.
jokes.explanation: The pomposity of 'just doesn't happen overnight' applied to retail window dressing is good irony. Lisa treats this like a martial arts progression or a sacred craft, which deflates Abbi completely. Economy is the main virtue.

jokes.text: Plus, you're an amazing sales associate! I c... I can't afford to lose you from the floor.
jokes.setup: Lisa has just told Abbi she's not ready for the display role.
jokes.punchline: She immediately pivots to praising Abbi's current role — the 'amazing sales associate' compliment is a transparently patronizing retention strategy, and 'I can't afford to lose you from the floor' frames Abbi's ambition as a threat to the menial workforce.
jokes.explanation: A recognizable workplace dynamic — the boss who praises you into your box. The humor is in how hollow and slightly demeaning the praise is. Works primarily as cringe recognition comedy for anyone who's worked retail.

jokes.text: Aaaah! [scream upon entering Jaimé's room]
jokes.setup: Ilana has been building up to entering Jaimé's forbidden room. There's a long gap — she presumably opens the door.
jokes.punchline: The primal scream of horror, with a gap of about 11 seconds before it (from [04:28] marker to [04:39]) suggesting she stood in the doorway processing the scene before screaming — the delayed reaction amplifies the horror.
jokes.explanation: The gap before the scream is the real joke — it implies she opened the door, her eyes traveled slowly across the room, and only then did her brain process what she was seeing. The scream itself is generic but the timing structure is good. On rewatch the gap is funnier.

jokes.text: A long time ago, I promised Jaimé I would never enter his room. / Okay, wait. Sorry. The whole time that you've lived here, you've never gone in Jaimé's room before? / Correct. He asked me not to, so I wasn't about to be another greedy-ass white person who thinks they're owed dominion over every single brown person's territory. Who do you think I am, some sort of Christina Columbus?
jokes.setup: Ilana explains why she never entered Jaimé's room, framing basic roommate respect as a political act.
jokes.punchline: The escalation from 'he asked me not to' → 'greedy-ass white person' → 'dominion over every single brown person's territory' → 'Christina Columbus' is a masterclass in Ilana's brand of hyper-woke self-awareness that's simultaneously sincere and absurd. The invented portmanteau 'Christina Columbus' (Christopher Columbus feminized) is the kicker — she's comparing entering a roommate's bedroom to European colonization.
jokes.explanation: This is the episode's best joke and one of the series' best. 'Christina Columbus' is an instantly quotable invention that captures everything about Ilana's character: she's politically conscious, over-the-top, and genuinely self-aware, but also completely unhinged in her framing. The escalation is perfectly calibrated — each step is more extreme than the last, culminating in a made-up historical figure. The character integration is near-perfect: only Ilana would frame not entering a roommate's bedroom as an act of decolonization. The wordplay of feminizing Columbus is clever. Comparable to the '8.0 anchor' — genuinely exceptional.

jokes.text: Okay, but then today I smelled a smell so bad, it was the stench of death.
jokes.setup: Ilana continues explaining why she finally broke her vow.
jokes.punchline: The tautological 'smelled a smell' is unintentionally (or deliberately) funny, and 'the stench of death' is a Gothic overcorrection that sets up the later reveal that it's just a half-eaten bagel.
jokes.explanation: The 'stench of death' is setup for the bagel payoff later, which retroactively makes it funnier on rewatch. On its own it's modest — the tautology 'smelled a smell' is a small verbal gag. Functions better as setup than standalone joke.

jokes.text: Like, have you ever seen 'American Psycho'? / Yeah. / Or 'Sicario'? / No, I didn't see that. / Or Matt Damon try to talk about sexual assault? / 'Cause this is worse.
jokes.setup: Ilana is warning Abbi about the horror of what she discovered in Jaimé's room.
jokes.punchline: The escalating list of disturbing references — American Psycho → Sicario → Matt Damon talking about sexual assault — with each item on the list being a different category of horror (fictional violence, real violence, celebrity tone-deafness), culminating in 'this is worse.' The Matt Damon reference is the pivot from fictional to real-world cultural disgust and it lands as the sharpest item.
jokes.explanation: The list structure is excellent — the escalating reference types (horror movie, crime thriller, real celebrity controversy) create rhythm, and the intrusion of Matt Damon into a list of fictional horrors is a sharp comedic pivot. The implicit joke is that Matt Damon's embarrassing #MeToo-adjacent comments (where he downplayed sexual assault spectrum arguments) was so cringe-inducing it belongs in a horror list. 'This is worse' applied to a hoarding situation is good misdirection. Strong structure, very Broad City in its tonal range.

jokes.text: Jaimé is a hoarder. [long beat of Abbi processing]
jokes.setup: After elaborate buildup involving American Psycho, Sicario, and Matt Damon, the reveal is imminent.
jokes.punchline: The reveal is 'Jaimé is a hoarder' — not a body, not a crime scene, not anything matching the horror-movie buildup. The subsequent ~25 seconds of silence ([05:54] to [06:19]) is presumably Abbi's slow visual scan of the room, letting the mundane reality sink in.
jokes.explanation: The misdirection setup (American Psycho, etc.) against the mundane reveal (hoarding) is well-executed. The long gap after the announcement suggests Abbi doing a slow visual survey of a cluttered room — the silence is the joke. The reveal works as comic deflation of the horror-movie buildup, which is a classic Broad City move: maximum buildup, minimum-danger payoff.

jokes.text: I mean, who doesn't finish an everything bagel with cream cheese?
jokes.setup: Ilana has found the source of the smell — a half-eaten everything bagel with cream cheese that had rolled behind something.
jokes.punchline: Her outrage is focused not on the hoarding, not on the smell, but on the crime of leaving an everything bagel unfinished — a very New York-specific moral failing.
jokes.explanation: A great joke that works on two levels: (1) the specific NYC-Jewish food culture indignation at an abandoned everything bagel, and (2) Ilana's priorities being completely inverted — she's discovered a significant mental health issue but her outrage is about the bagel. 'Everything bagel with cream cheese' is specific enough to be funny on its own. 'Who doesn't finish' positions it as a moral failing. High character integration — this outrage is quintessentially Ilana and quintessentially New York.

jokes.text: Okay, Abbi, we don't call people 'crazy' 'cause it stigmatizes mental illness.
jokes.setup: Abbi says 'I know he's crazy' about the hoarding.
jokes.punchline: Ilana — who has been treating this discovery with maximum hysteria — calmly corrects Abbi's mental health language. The irony of Ilana being the voice of sensitivity here, after screaming 'the stench of death' and comparing it to American Psycho, is rich.
jokes.explanation: The comedy is in Ilana's inconsistency — she's been wildly histrionic but snaps into language-policing mode with perfect calm. Character-specific: Ilana holds both the performatively woke identity and the chaos-brain simultaneously without awareness of contradiction. This joke also sets up the callback payoff at the very end of the episode.

jokes.text: Abbi, denial ain't just a river in, um... / [long pause] / He's in denial.
jokes.setup: Ilana starts the classic 'denial ain't just a river in Egypt' joke.
jokes.punchline: She cannot remember the word 'Egypt' — a 6-second gap as she draws a complete blank — then gives up and just explains the joke literally: 'He's in denial.' The complete abandonment of the punchline to deliver a flat explanation is funnier than if she'd remembered Egypt.
jokes.explanation: This is a clever meta-joke about a cliché joke. Ilana begins the most tired pun in American comedy, then fails to complete it — the gap as she struggles to recall 'Egypt' is the actual punchline, a delicious subversion of the expectation. The literal explanation 'He's in denial' is even better: she dismantles the joke entirely and delivers the thesis statement with the same energy. The structure is genuinely inventive: set up a cliché → fail at the cliché → deliver the meaning without the wordplay. Very rewatchable because the gap before 'Egypt' gets funnier each time.

jokes.text: Ugh! She cannot talk to you like that. She's not the boss of you. / I mean, she literally is the boss of me. She's my boss.
jokes.setup: Ilana rallies to Abbi's defense against the dismissive boss.
jokes.punchline: Abbi matter-of-factly corrects Ilana: 'I mean, she literally is the boss of me. She's my boss.' — the tautological correction of Ilana's hyperbolic loyalty is a clean, efficient joke.
jokes.explanation: A textbook setup/punchline with great economy. Ilana's friendship loyalty overrides basic logical reality; Abbi's deadpan correction is exactly right. The repetition — 'literally is the boss of me. She's my boss' — adds a slightly baffled quality, as if Abbi can't believe she has to explain this. The joke is in Ilana's selective reality.

jokes.text: Wait. It is, like, so cuckoo that Jaimé kept all my psych textbooks from college.
jokes.setup: They're sifting through Jaimé's hoard.
jokes.punchline: Ilana finds her own psych textbooks in Jaimé's hoard — the irony that her textbooks are now being used to diagnose a situation happening around her own possessions, which she apparently never noticed were missing.
jokes.explanation: Good situational irony — Ilana's own psychology textbooks enabling the impromptu therapy session, found in the very hoard being addressed. Also: she didn't notice they were gone, suggesting she has no great relationship to academic materials. Character-consistent and well-timed.

jokes.text: Uh, Doy-ee! / Got it. Make you buy the book. Duh! We're, like... We... We know.
jokes.setup: Ilana reads from a psych textbook describing hoarding, and it perfectly describes Jaimé's situation.
jokes.punchline: Ilana and Abbi's reaction to an accurate clinical description — pretending they already knew, talking over each other with performed self-evidence — is a funny bit of performative competence hiding actual ignorance.
jokes.explanation: The joke is in the overcorrection — both women perform knowledge they clearly don't have after being enlightened by a textbook. 'Make you buy the book. Duh!' is an odd, funny deflection. Not the strongest joke but lands as character comedy.

jokes.text: 'Treatment... absolutely never, ever, under any circumstances... m-move the hoarder's possessions without them.' [beat, then cut to: 'All right. Think it matches? / Close enough, right?']
jokes.setup: Ilana reads the treatment section of the psych textbook, which advises against the one thing they're apparently doing.
jokes.punchline: Hard cut to them having already rearranged the entire room — the reading of the warning immediately followed by evidence they're violating it, presumably having been doing so the whole time. The 'Close enough, right?' about photo-matching the room's original state is the punchline.
jokes.explanation: This is an excellent structural joke. The comedy is entirely in the cut: we hear the warning 'never move the hoarder's possessions' and then immediately see that they've been doing exactly that. The editing is the punchline. 'Close enough, right?' about using a photo to restore a hoarder's room to its original state is great — it implies they took the precaution of documenting before moving things (smart!) but their restoration standards are hilariously casual. Strong structure: the textbook becomes the setup for its own immediate violation. The irony is crisp and well-earned.

jokes.text: So good you took this photo, dude. / Oh, my God, I needed to. My mother lives for this shit. Whenever she's on a diet and needs to stop herself from eating, she just Googles hoarder photos. Loses her appetite immediately.
jokes.setup: Abbi praises Ilana for taking a photo of the room's original state.
jokes.punchline: Ilana reveals the photo wasn't for restoration purposes — it was a hoarder photo for her dieting mother's appetite suppression. The utility of the photo is completely unrelated to their current mission.
jokes.explanation: This is a great joke with multiple layers. The surface comedy is the Rube Goldberg chain: Ilana's mom → dieting → appetite suppression → Googling hoarder photos → Ilana documenting hoarder rooms for her. The deeper comedy is that the photo that 'saves' them was taken for an entirely unrelated and darkly absurd purpose. The detail of 'loses her appetite immediately' is specific and strange enough to be very funny. High character integration: only Ilana would have a mother with this particular coping mechanism, and only Ilana would share it casually. Strong originality.

jokes.text: Okay. Hola, Abbi. / Hi. Hey. / Wait. I think I'm gonna go. / No! You helped me decorate my whole office. You don't want to see the magic happen in it?
jokes.setup: The 'therapy session' is set up. Johnny immediately tries to leave.
jokes.punchline: Ilana guilts Johnny into staying by invoking that he helped decorate the 'office' — which is her apartment living room, now pretentiously reframed as a therapeutic space. 'You don't want to see the magic happen in it?' is delightfully self-important.
jokes.explanation: Decent character comedy — Ilana's grandiosity about her improvised therapy setup is consistent. 'See the magic happen' has good pomposity. Not exceptional but functional.

jokes.text: Okay, listen, I thought that I could do this, but when my parents got divorced, I had to go through a lot of therapy, and this is actually very triggering for me. I'm not good with therapy. / Okay. If you stay, you can have some of the cheese.
jokes.setup: Abbi has a sincere emotional disclosure about therapy being triggering for her.
jokes.punchline: Ilana's response to a genuine emotional moment is to bribe Abbi with cheese. The bathos — serious trauma disclosure met with a cheese offer — is the joke, and 'some of the cheese' is funny because it implies Ilana has already been eating it.
jokes.explanation: The cheese bribe as response to a therapy trauma disclosure is good bathos. The economy is excellent — Ilana doesn't miss a beat. 'You can have some of the cheese' implies she's been snacking through this whole setup, which is a nice incidental detail. Character-specific: Ilana's empathy is real but frequently overridden by food and chaos.

jokes.text: I want to assure you I would never share what happens in this room with anybody in my personal life. / But is that because, like, most of the people in your personal life are, like, in this room right now? / Mm. So true. See, we are already making some excellent discoveries here.
jokes.setup: Ilana opens the 'therapy session' with a confidentiality assurance.
jokes.punchline: Jaimé immediately exposes the confidentiality pledge as hollow — her entire social circle is in the room — and Ilana enthusiastically reframes this devastating observation as a 'therapy discovery.'
jokes.explanation: This is a tight three-beat joke: (1) Ilana performs professional confidentiality, (2) Jaimé dismantles it with a simple observation about her limited social world, (3) Ilana co-opts the observation as clinical evidence of discovery. The third beat is the best — Ilana's ability to absorb any criticism and reframe it as validation is perfectly in character and very funny. 'We are already making some excellent discoveries here' is quotable.

jokes.text: I had to go in your room because I smelled what I thought could only be the stench of death. But then I later discovered it was an unfinished everything bagel with cream cheese, which... no judgment... is way worse.
jokes.setup: Ilana explains to Jaimé why she violated his privacy.
jokes.punchline: The callback to the bagel, with 'no judgment' inserted in the middle of a judgment, followed by the sincere belief that abandoning an everything bagel is 'way worse' than 'the stench of death.' The escalation of bagel abandonment above death on the moral hierarchy is perfect.
jokes.explanation: This is a strong callback that improves on the original joke. 'No judgment' inserted mid-judgment is a classic irony structure, but the real punch is 'way worse' — Ilana sincerely ranks leaving an everything bagel above the stench of death on the scale of moral offenses. The specificity ('with cream cheese') makes it funnier. This is quintessential Broad City: a sincere, specific, absurd food-as-moral-compass joke.

jokes.text: I think it's going well. / Really good. / Yeah.
jokes.setup: The therapy session has just had Jaimé storm out in anger.
jokes.punchline: Ilana and Abbi mutually agree the session is going well, with Abbi's 'Yeah' adding enthusiastic agreement — the delusion is shared and therefore more complete.
jokes.explanation: Perfect deadpan irony. They've just watched Jaimé storm out of a therapy session he didn't consent to, and both agree it's going 'really good.' The brevity and shared delusion make it funnier than a solo reaction would be. Economy is its main virtue.

jokes.text: Hold on. Ilana, are you using the same cheese grater on the cheese that you're using on your feet?
jokes.setup: Ilana has had a cheese plate at the therapy session and has apparently been using a cheese grater on her feet between scenes.
jokes.punchline: The implication that a single cheese grater serves both podiatric and culinary functions in Ilana's home — setting up the subsequent foot-cheese arc.
jokes.explanation: This is a very good visual/cringe joke that launches an extended running gag. The specificity of a cheese grater — the most cheese-specific instrument possible — being used on feet and then on actual cheese is maximally disgusting. Abbi's delivery as a calm, clarifying question rather than a scream of horror is the right comedic choice. Sets up multiple payoffs. High character integration: only Ilana's household would have this ambiguity.

jokes.text: I'm sorry, I'm still feeling very uncomfortable in general, and I'm also starting to feel ill, probably because I've been eating cheese that's mixed in with your heel skin.
jokes.setup: The therapy session resumes; Abbi has been eating the possibly-contaminated cheese.
jokes.punchline: Abbi has continued eating the cheese throughout, and only now is she acknowledging potential illness — the passive horror that she kept eating it despite her suspicion.
jokes.explanation: Good escalation — Abbi's careful articulation of 'heel skin' mixed into cheese is deliberately disgusting. The implied horror is that she kept eating. 'Mixed in with your heel skin' is a specific, anatomically precise phrase that maximizes the gross-out. Functional callback.

jokes.text: I put the grater in Barbicide after I use it on my feet. Do you think I'm an idiot? / Barbicide?
jokes.setup: Abbi expresses concern about eating foot-cheese.
jokes.punchline: Ilana reveals she sterilizes the grater in Barbicide — the blue disinfectant liquid used in barbershops for combs. She considers this a complete sanitary solution. Abbi's one-word response 'Barbicide?' captures the inadequacy of this reassurance.
jokes.explanation: One of the episode's best jokes. 'Barbicide' is a genius specific detail — it's a real product (the blue liquid barbers use for sterilizing combs), and Ilana using it to justify cross-contaminating a cheese grater is both insane and somehow internally logical. The fact that she has Barbicide in her apartment, uses it on kitchen tools after foot grooming, and considers this full sanitary clearance is a deep character detail delivered with complete confidence. 'Do you think I'm an idiot?' makes it better — she's genuinely offended at the implication of unsanitary practice. Abbi's one-word 'Barbicide?' is a perfect reaction: not quite a full objection, just a baffled echo. High originality — I don't know another joke that has used Barbicide as a false-sanitation reassurance.

jokes.text: I'm sorry, but, Ilana, do I have to go to the hospital?
jokes.setup: The foot cheese discussion continues while Jaimé is sharing real emotional content about his relationship.
jokes.punchline: Abbi's interruption of Jaimé's genuine emotional breakthrough with a hospital inquiry — perfectly timed to the worst possible moment in the session.
jokes.explanation: The timing is the joke — Abbi's interjection comes exactly at the point of maximum emotional sincerity from Jaimé. The escalation from 'feeling ill' to 'do I have to go to the hospital?' suggests continued eating of the cheese and worsening imagined symptoms. Good structural comedy from the interruption.

jokes.text: Okay, you are wasting Jaimé's hour. Okay? This is so unprofessional! I'll handle it, okay?
jokes.setup: Ilana responds to Abbi's hospital question during the therapy session.
jokes.punchline: Ilana calling someone else 'unprofessional' while running an illegal therapy session using her own psych textbooks in her living room, with contaminated cheese on the table.
jokes.explanation: The irony of 'unprofessional' in this context is rich. Ilana has constructed an elaborate fiction of professional competence and fiercely defends it. Good character integration — her commitment to the bit overrides all self-awareness.

jokes.text: Okay, dude, just be honest with me. Did I or did I not eat your foot cheese? / What part of 'I put the cheese grater in Barbicide before it touches the cheese' do you not understand?
jokes.setup: Ilana has taken Abbi aside to talk; Abbi is still fixated on the foot cheese.
jokes.punchline: The formal phrasing 'Did I or did I not eat your foot cheese' — treating this as a legal question requiring definitive adjudication — and Ilana's exasperated re-explanation of the Barbicide protocol.
jokes.explanation: 'Did I or did I not eat your foot cheese' is quotable — it has the formal gravity of a courtroom question applied to the grossest possible subject. 'Foot cheese' as a compound noun is doing a lot of work here. Diminishing returns on the Barbicide explanation, but the emotional weight Abbi puts on this question is funny.

jokes.text: And not for nothing, you're probably lactose-intolerant 'cause you're an Ashkenazi Jew. Or maybe you didn't know that. Okay?
jokes.setup: Ilana continues dismissing Abbi's foot cheese concerns.
jokes.punchline: Ilana pivots to diagnosing Abbi's gastrointestinal distress as Ashkenazi Jewish lactose intolerance — a real statistical correlation deployed with Ilana's characteristic breezy confidence in her own unsolicited medical advice, topped with 'Or maybe you didn't know that' as a condescending flourish.
jokes.explanation: The specificity of 'Ashkenazi Jew' in the context of lactose intolerance is both medically accurate (Ashkenazi Jews do have higher rates of lactose intolerance) and absurdly confident. Ilana deploying ethno-medical trivia as a way to deflect foot cheese responsibility is very on-brand. 'Or maybe you didn't know that' is a condescending closer that characterizes her perfectly. Good character integration.

jokes.text: And have you never sucked on a couple toes before? / I have sucked on toes. / Really? / Yeah. / Because you're lucky, girl. A lot of people pay good money for my foot shavings.
jokes.setup: Ilana continues her case for why foot cheese is no big deal.
jokes.punchline: The escalating sequence: (1) Ilana normalizes toe-sucking, (2) Abbi casually admits to sucking toes, (3) Ilana reveals she has a commercial market for her foot shavings — people paying money for Ilana's foot skin is a detail so specific and committed it becomes genuinely funny.
jokes.explanation: The three-beat escalation here is well-constructed. Abbi's casual 'I have sucked on toes' is funny precisely because she doesn't hesitate — it's not a confession, just a fact. But the real punchline is 'a lot of people pay good money for my foot shavings' — which implies an actual cottage industry in Ilana's foot skin, operating presumably through some informal marketplace. This is deeply Ilana: she has commodified a body product most people consider waste material, and she's proud of it. High character integration and originality.

jokes.text: Mi amor, I decided to move in with Johnny, in his house in New Jersey.
jokes.setup: Jaimé thanks Ilana for making him confront his hoarding, framing the session as a breakthrough.
jokes.punchline: The breakthrough is: moving to New Jersey. After all the emotional processing, the conclusion is geographic relocation to the state that New Yorkers consider culturally beneath them.
jokes.explanation: The New Jersey reveal works as a comedic anticlimax — the grand breakthrough is a move to the most mocked state in the tri-state area. Functions mainly as setup for Ilana's explosion.

jokes.text: No. No, no, no, no, no, no, no, no, no, no. / Where the fuck is the reverse-psychology section?! Where is it?!
jokes.setup: Jaimé announces he's moving to New Jersey with Johnny.
jokes.punchline: Ilana immediately abandons her therapist persona and starts frantically searching the psych textbook for reverse psychology techniques — the complete collapse of the professional facade into raw panic.
jokes.explanation: The physical desperation of frantically searching for a 'reverse psychology section' is very funny — both because it shows she was weaponizing the session all along, and because her faith in the textbook as a practical manipulation manual is absurd. The ten 'no's before the line are good physical comedy — a raw panic response. High character integration: Ilana's true motivation (keeping her living situation intact) erupts through the therapeutic facade.

jokes.text: Jaimé, I don't like to say 'crazy,' but you are fuckin' nuts, okay? Moving to Jersey?! What are you, Bruce Springsteen?! / You're like... ♪ Henh, henh, huhhhggh, eghghh ♪
jokes.setup: Ilana is horrified by the New Jersey announcement.
jokes.punchline: She invokes Bruce Springsteen as the quintessential New Jersey person, then attempts to impersonate him — producing a series of guttural sounds that barely resemble music. The callback to 'I don't like to say crazy' (which she used to correct Abbi earlier) while immediately calling Jaimé 'fuckin' nuts' is also a nice callback.
jokes.explanation: Multiple layers: (1) the callback to her own mental-illness-language policing being immediately violated is sharp irony, (2) the Bruce Springsteen reference as shorthand for New Jersey is culturally specific, and (3) the Springsteen 'impression' — apparently just guttural groaning — is very funny physical comedy. The impersonation that follows (repeated at [15:55]) suggests Ilana has absolutely no idea what Springsteen sounds like, which is its own characterization joke. The 'I don't like to say crazy but you are fuckin' nuts' construction is also good self-contradiction.

jokes.text: Wait. Where's Mon-claya? / It's 'Montclair.' / Montclair!
jokes.setup: Johnny says it's easy — they'll be just across in Montclair.
jokes.punchline: Ilana mispronounces 'Montclair' as 'Mon-claya' — either an affectation or genuine ignorance — then enthusiastically repeats the correct pronunciation as if she's just learned a foreign word.
jokes.explanation: A light character moment — Ilana's mispronunciation of a New Jersey suburb and her delight in learning the correct version. The 'Mon-claya' affectation implies she either doesn't know NJ geography at all or is doing an unnecessary accent. Mild laugh, good for the scene's emotional reset.

jokes.text: Um, so, who is this Bruce Springsteen again? / Yeah, I don't really know his music. / You don't know who The Boss is? / Mnh-mnh.
jokes.setup: After Ilana's passionate Springsteen invocation, Jaimé and Johnny reveal they don't know who Bruce Springsteen is.
jokes.punchline: The irony that Ilana's impassioned New Jersey/Springsteen rant was made to people with no frame of reference for the reference — and Ilana's baffled 'You don't know who The Boss is?' sets up the final Springsteen bit.
jokes.explanation: Good situational irony — Ilana's cultural reference landed on people who have no cultural context for it. The specificity of 'The Boss' as Springsteen's nickname adds a layer of Ilana's serious investment in this. Sets up the impersonation payoff.

jokes.text: What does he sound like? / Yeah. / ♪ Hey, mm, hey, he-hoo-ho ♪ / ♪ Hey, mm, hey, hm, hey, hmm-hmm ♪ / ♪ Hey, mm, baby! ♪ / Right. I-I now get the vibe. / You got it? / Ah, yes. / I get the vibe.
jokes.setup: Jaimé asks what Bruce Springsteen sounds like.
jokes.punchline: Ilana's Springsteen 'impression' consists entirely of guttural syllables and exclamations with no recognizable melody — yet Jaimé and Johnny confirm they 'get the vibe.' The shared delusion that this is an accurate representation of Springsteen is the joke.
jokes.explanation: The impression itself is the joke — Ilana apparently believes Springsteen's entire musical identity can be conveyed through 'henh, henh, huhhhggh.' The callback from earlier in the episode extends the bit and the confirmation from Jaimé ('I get the vibe') is the perfect capper — everyone agrees on a shared fiction. Physical comedy in performance; rewatchable for the specific syllables Ilana chooses.

jokes.text: Hey, Johnny. Can I ask you something? / Yeah. / Am I a good therapist?
jokes.setup: The emotional resolution of the Jaimé situation is happening.
jokes.punchline: Ilana, after running an illegal, contaminated, textbook-improvised therapy session that nearly fell apart multiple times, sincerely asks the one person present (Johnny, the most tangential participant) if she's a good therapist.
jokes.explanation: The sincerity of the question is what makes it funny — Ilana genuinely wants validation. Choosing Johnny as the authority on this is an odd choice (he barely participated), which adds to the comedy. The earnestness characterizes Ilana: she wants professional recognition for a completely non-professional act.

jokes.text: No matter how wildly unprofessional you were, you made the space for Jaimé to talk about his issues. That's really all you can do as a therapist, right? Just make the space.
jokes.setup: Johnny answers Ilana's question about whether she's a good therapist.
jokes.punchline: He begins with 'no matter how wildly unprofessional you were' — an immediate, frank concession that she was wildly unprofessional — before pivoting to a genuine compliment. The brutal honesty of 'wildly unprofessional' as a qualifier is the joke.
jokes.explanation: The 'wildly unprofessional' concession is the only real joke here; the rest is genuine emotional payoff. It's a brief character moment for Johnny — the most grounded person in the room — to call out Ilana's chaos while still being generous. Functional, not exceptional.

jokes.text: 'Make the space.' I need to make the space in Anthropologie. No one's gonna be like, 'Hey, Abbi, you want to do a window display?' No, I got to fuckin' do it myself.
jokes.setup: Abbi hears 'make the space' as therapeutic advice.
jokes.punchline: She immediately applies it to her retail career ambitions — a leap from therapeutic philosophy to guerrilla window display design. The decision to DIY a corporate retail display is both inspiring and clearly going to go wrong.
jokes.explanation: Functional plot-advancement joke. The comedy is mild dramatic irony — we know this plan is going to be a disaster. Character-consistent: Abbi's impulsivity channeled through sudden inspiration is a series trope.

jokes.text: What was that, Abbi? What did you call it? / Sorry, did I say... Did I say 'trash'? I meant 'precious objects.' I can use all these beautiful, precious objects... to make the display.
jokes.setup: Abbi refers to Jaimé's hoarded items as 'trash' to use in her display.
jokes.punchline: Ilana catches 'trash' and demands a correction; Abbi immediately revises to 'precious objects' with theatrical sincerity, demonstrating she's absorbed the hoarding-sensitivity framing while also completely planning to use the items.
jokes.explanation: Good callback — the sensitivity toward Jaimé's 'precious objects' (established in the hoarding discussion) gets deployed as a verbal correction while Abbi simultaneously plans to raid them. The pause before 'I meant precious objects' is a nice beat. Character-specific for both: Ilana's language-policing and Abbi's adaptable social performance.

jokes.text: Follow your third eye... your clit.
jokes.setup: Ilana gives Abbi inspirational advice about following her artistic instincts.
jokes.punchline: She begins with the spiritual cliché 'follow your third eye' then revises it to 'your clit' — positioning the clitoris as the site of intuitive artistic wisdom.
jokes.explanation: This is a quintessential Ilana Glazer line — maybe the most quotable joke in the episode outside 'Christina Columbus.' The structure is simple: begin with new-age spirituality ('third eye') and land on female anatomy with complete sincerity. The genius is that it's not a dirty joke — Ilana means it. The clitoris as the seat of artistic intuition is absurd but delivered with the earnestness of genuine life advice. Maximum economy (six words), maximum character integration (only Ilana says this), genuinely original. This is one of those lines that feels like it was discovered in improv and is perfect.

jokes.text: Christina, come on! You got to let me see my baby boy! Of course I'm responsible enough to be a dad! / Ohh. Christina! Nasty. Don't hang up! / You're not listening. You're not Li... Christina! You're not listening!
jokes.setup: A man in an Anthropologie is having a desperate phone call with his baby mama while surrounded by bohemian retail decor.
jokes.punchline: The juxtaposition of the emotional paternity crisis ('let me see my baby boy') with the Anthropologie setting, plus the man's complete obliviousness to his surroundings, and the escalating desperation as Christina refuses to engage. 'Nasty' as a reaction to being hung up on is a good specific detail.
jokes.explanation: The comedy is primarily environmental — a very human drama (paternity dispute) happening in a very manicured retail space. The man's passionate advocacy for his parental fitness surrounded by sage smudging wands and Caucasian headdresses is a good juxtaposition. 'Nasty' as the response to being hung up on is a nice specific detail. Not a sophisticated joke but gets a reaction from the contrast.

jokes.text: Good morning, bitch. / Jesus! / Ilana, what? / Yeah, I guess I got carried away.
jokes.setup: It's morning; Abbi comes to see the finished window display.
jokes.punchline: Ilana is apparently IN the window display — she's installed herself as part of the art piece. 'Good morning, bitch' delivered from inside a store window at dawn, followed by 'Yeah, I guess I got carried away' as a mild understatement about spending the night in a retail display.
jokes.explanation: The visual of Ilana inside the display is the main laugh — 'Good morning, bitch' from inside a store window is an excellent cold-open for the scene. 'Yeah, I guess I got carried away' as understatement about overnight retail installation is perfect deadpan. High character integration: Ilana's commitment to a project manifests as physically inhabiting it. Very rewatchable as a visual.

jokes.text: You motherfuckers. This is insane. You know what? Zoe, you're the fucking worst. I dare you to find someone who can fold a tunic sweater better than me!
jokes.setup: The Anthropologie response to the guerrilla display — presumably they're firing/banning Ilana.
jokes.punchline: Ilana's parting shot isn't about the display or the ban — it's a challenge about her tunic-sweater-folding superiority. The non-sequitur assertion of retail excellence as a final stand is perfect: she's being fired/banned for putting a hoarder's room in a store window, and her dignity is staked on sweater-folding.
jokes.explanation: A great character joke. Ilana has just been banned from every Anthropologie in the country for putting a rat and hoarder debris in a window, and her final act of defiance is to assert her tunic sweater-folding skills. The specificity of 'tunic sweater' is perfect — it's such an Anthropologie product. The shift from artistic outrage to retail pride is the joke: Ilana can't help taking pride in the menial job she's also rebelling against. 'Zoe, you're the fucking worst' is a nice specific address to an unnamed coworker. Very quotable.

jokes.text: Oh, my God! / What? / Rat Bastard! / Oh, my God! Ilana, what?! / Okay, I-I put a rodent and hoarder trash in their window.
jokes.setup: Ilana and Abbi pose for a photo in front of the display.
jokes.punchline: The reveal that the display included an actual rodent (named 'Rat Bastard') — and Ilana's matter-of-fact explanation 'I put a rodent and hoarder trash in their window' delivered as if this is the obvious conclusion of events.
jokes.explanation: The name 'Rat Bastard' is excellent — it treats the rat as a collaborator or beloved pet with a character name. The deadpan recounting 'I put a rodent and hoarder trash in their window' is perfect because it's both a total admission and delivered without any sense that this was wrong. The rewatch value is high because the display presumably contains visual details that make this funnier on second view.

jokes.text: I think they actually made the right call.
jokes.setup: Ilana explains she put a rat and hoarder debris in an Anthropologie window.
jokes.punchline: Abbi's completely reasonable, perfectly timed concession that the company made the right call in removing this — a rare moment of Abbi being the reality-anchor.
jokes.explanation: Maximum economy — six words that perfectly acknowledge the situation. Abbi's willingness to side with the corporation against her best friend on this one is funny. The understatement of 'made the right call' for removing a rat installation is good deadpan.

jokes.text: Damn. First Jaimé, and now Rat Bastard. Looks like all my roommates are moving out.
jokes.setup: Ilana processes the consequences — Jaimé moving out, and now the rat being removed from the window.
jokes.punchline: She equates Jaimé moving to New Jersey with Rat Bastard being expelled from a retail window display, treating a rat as a co-equal roommate. The flat equation of the two losses is the joke.
jokes.explanation: The elegance of this joke is the parallel construction: 'First Jaimé, and now Rat Bastard.' The implied grief over a rat's eviction from a store window is funny because Ilana is treating it with the same emotional weight as a roommate departure. 'Looks like all my roommates are moving out' frames this as a crisis of shared living, which is absurd and also surprisingly touching. Strong character integration and quotability.

jokes.text: Yo, you know what? It's pretty badass to be banned from every Anthropologie in the country. Like, not many people can say that.
jokes.setup: Ilana is processing the consequences of the display.
jokes.punchline: Abbi reframes a national retail ban as an achievement — a marker of distinction that 'not many people can say.' The logic is absurd but the comfort is genuine.
jokes.explanation: Functional friendship comedy — Abbi's reframing of humiliation as distinction is a recurring feature of their dynamic. 'Not many people can say that' is the best line. Mild but warm.

jokes.text: Like, obviously in addition to my Phone Wigs hustle.
jokes.setup: Ilana mentions she's considering pursuing therapy as a career.
jokes.punchline: 'Phone Wigs' — an unexplained business concept Ilana apparently runs — casually inserted as her primary income source, with therapy being the supplemental career. The confidence with which 'Phone Wigs' is invoked as a real, ongoing enterprise is the joke.
jokes.explanation: 'Phone Wigs' is a perfect Broad City absurdist invention — two words that imply an entire business concept (wigs for phones? phones shaped like wigs?) that is never explained and doesn't need to be. The confidence with which Ilana references it as an ongoing 'hustle' is the joke. This is the kind of detail that rewards rewatching because you want to understand what Phone Wigs is but also know you'll never be told. High originality, maximum economy, extremely high character integration. It's not a traditional joke — it's a world-building absurdist detail that functions as comedy.

jokes.text: Ooh, my mom is gonna flip out.
jokes.setup: Ilana considers telling her mom she might pursue therapy school.
jokes.punchline: Given what we know of Ilana's mom (she Googles hoarder photos for appetite suppression), the idea of 'my mom is gonna flip out' at the relatively conventional news of grad school is mildly funny as a character reference.
jokes.explanation: A mild callback to the established mom character. Works mostly as a warm beat rather than a strong joke. Below average for the episode.

jokes.text: Oh, my God. We might have to stop our sessions because I can't be in love with my favorite patient.
jokes.setup: Abbi has revealed that Ilana is her therapist
jokes.punchline: Ilana immediately adopts the ethical concern of therapist-patient romantic boundaries — as if the entire fake therapy was always a legitimate professional relationship that now faces a real ethical challenge
jokes.explanation: A perfect closing line for the therapy arc. Ilana immediately — and with complete sincerity — worries about the ethical implications of being in love with her patient, which is both a sweet statement of their friendship ('I can't be in love with my favorite patient') and an absurd escalation of the fake therapist bit. The line also activates the show's recurring romantic friendship energy between Abbi and Ilana. Strong closing joke.

jokes.text: Yeah. And the shoulders. The shoulders just go... [physical gesture/movement] / There it is. There it is!
jokes.setup: Post-credits or tag scene; Ilana appears to be doing a Springsteen impression or some physical characterization
jokes.punchline: Whatever physical movement she does, the enthusiastic 'There it is. There it is!' implies she has finally nailed something — likely a Springsteen shoulder move — with tremendous self-satisfaction
jokes.explanation: A tag that callbacks the Springsteen bit. The comedy is inferred — presumably a physical gesture — with 'There it is!' as someone finally achieving a physical impression they've been working on. Mild but charming closing beat that rewards the Springsteen thread.
